summary: O'Neil argues that official COVID-19 statistics are systematically undercounting both cases and deaths due to inadequate testing and inconsistent reporting. She estimates actual US cases are roughly 10x the confirmed count, based on the logic that only the sickest 10% get tested. She notes that death counts are also unreliable, with nursing home deaths excluded in some countries and China's numbers particularly suspect. The post reflects the frustration of a data scientist who fundamentally distrusts the available data while trying to reason through what the true numbers might be.
key_points: New York's testing requirement of near-hospitalization means confirmed cases represent only about 10% of actual infections
Multiplying confirmed US cases by 10 suggests 1.43 million actual cases versus the official 143K
Death counts are underestimated because they exclude nursing home deaths in countries like Spain and Italy
China's case and death numbers are particularly untrustworthy, which matters because we need to understand their post-lockdown trajectory
Being a skeptical data scientist means not trusting official data and using real-world knowledge to estimate reality
money_quotes: Long story short, being a skeptical data scientist means not trusting the data whatsoever.
The best we can do is use the data and our real world knowledge to ballpark what might actually be happening. We will never know the true numbers.
All the above are ballpark reckoning, but honestly I trust my numbers more than any official ones.
We've been hearing stories told in Spain and Italy that their death counts are not including horrible fucking things that have been happening in nursing homes.
And if we don't trust their death counts, we should also not count their confirmed case count.
I could be wrong about that too.
key_insight: Official pandemic statistics are so compromised by testing limitations and political incentives that back-of-envelope estimates based on hospitalization rates may be more accurate than reported numbers.
